Facts.text: Darius I of Persia kills the Magian usurper Gaumâta, securing his hold as king of the Persian Empire
Battle of Salamis: The Greek fleet under Themistocles defeats the Persian fleet under Xerxes I
Batavian soldiers consecrate altar on Hercules Magusanus Rome
St Pontianus ends his reign as Catholic Pope
Pope Leo I the Great, installed
Benedict III begins his reign as Catholic Pope
Pope Gregory IX excommunicate German emperor Frederick II
People of Krems Austria accuse Jews of poisoning wells
Battle of Auray, English forces defeat French in Brittany
Robert Dudley becomes Earl of Leicester
At a dinner, the Duke of Alba arrests the Count of Egmont and the Count of Hoorn for treason
Treaty of Plessis-lez-Tours signed between Duke of Anjou and Dutch Staten-Generaal
Captain Christopher Newport arrives in Virginia, for the second time, with supplies from England for colonists
2nd Fronde, the Fronde of the Princes uprising ends in France
English merchant Henry Robinson opens the first marriage bureau (dating agency) on Threadneedle Street in London
English expedition to Hudson Bay led by Frenchman Médard Chouart Des Groseilliers arrives at Nemiscau (Rupert) River aboard the Nonsuch (beginning of Hudson Bay Company)
France & Brandenburg sign peace treaty
Williamsburg Bray School, now the oldest extant building dedicated to the education of Black children in the United States, opens in Williamsburg, Virginia
Chasidic sect is excommunicated in Kraków, Poland
US War Department establishes a regular army
Tennis is first mentioned in an English sporting magazine
Dutch King William I forms the Dutch Order of the Lion, oldest and highest civilian order of chivalry in the Netherlands
Battle of Pákozd: Hungarian forces defeat Croats at Pákozd; the first battle of the War of Independence
1st passenger train service to Peekskill, New York (New Haven Railroad)
Emigrant ship "Annie Jane" sinks off Scotland, drowning 348
Great auroral display in the US
Battle of Chaffin's Farm and New Market Heights in Henrico County, Virginia
Kiowa-chief Lone Wolf captures Satanta & Big Tree
NL owners meeting in Buffalo adopt reserve clause, giving each team exclusive rights to their players
1st pro baseball game at Polo Grounds, NY Metropolitans beat Washington Nationals 4-2 in 5 innings
First practical public electric tramway in the world is opened in Blackpool, England
Wassoulou Empire leader Samori Ture captured by French troops in West Africa ending his 20 year rule
American Veterans of Foreign Service, later known as Veterans of Foreign War (VFW), support group is established in Columbus, Ohio by a group of Spanish-American war veterans
Impresario David Belasco opens his 1st Broadway theater
1st monument honoring Spanish–American War erected, in Monroeville, Ohio
US intervenes in Cuba ousts dictator Estrada Palma
Construction begins on Washington National Cathedral
Calgary Rugby Football Union forms
National Urban League founded as Committee on Urban Conditions Among Negroes by Ruth Standish Baldwin and Dr. George Edmund Haynes in New York City
MLB Washington SenatorWalter Johnsonwins his 36th game
Shubert Theater, named after Sam S. Shubert, opens at 225 W 44th St NYC
The Treaty of Constantinople between Turkey and Bulgaria restores peace; the Turks recover Adrianople and Maritza River line
Boston Braves, who were in last place in mid-July, clinch NL pennant
1st transcontinental radio telephone message sent by U.S. naval radio station at Arlington, Virginia. to naval radio station at Mare Island, San Francisco
British army conquers Chilly al Imara, Mesopotamia
Dutch Opera's 1st performance
Herbert/Blossoms musical "Princess Pat" premieres in NYC
Hurricane claims 275 in Mississippi Delta
Philadelphia Phillies clinch their 1st pennant
WWI: Allied forces score a decisive breakthrough of Hindenburg Line
Belgium annexes Eupen/Malmö dy
Joseph Horne Company in Pittsburgh sells radios for $10
MLB Pittsburgh Pirates drop doubleheader to St. Louis Cardinals, allowing idle New York Giants to clinch NL
British Mandate of Palestine, sanctioned by the League of Nation in 1920, officially begins
Steinhart Aquarium opens to public in Golden Gate Park, San Francisco, California
The British Empire reaches its geographical peak, covering a quarter of globe's land (nearly fourteen million square miles), with the Palestine Mandate coming into force under British control
MLB Washington Senators clinch pennant, finishing 2 games in front of New York Yankees
Santo Domingo joins League of Nations
French General of Morocco, Marshal Lyautey, is dismissed
Greek republican constitution enforced
Telephone service begins between US & Mexico
Tornado strikes St Louis, Missouri, 85 die
Yankees sluggerBabe Ruthties MLB record by hitting grand slams in consecutive games; New York's 7-4 win v Philadelphia A's and 15-4 rout of Washington Senators
Cardinals win NL pennant with a 3-1 win at Boston
Yanks (17) Tigers (28) set 9 inning hit record (45)-Tigers win 19-10
1st Canadian football game played under lights, Hamilton-UBC
Boquerón battle ends Paraguay border dispute
Lowell Thomas makes his debut on CBS Radio replacing Floyd Gibbons
NYC College offers 1st course in radio advertising
Little King, Cartoon Character, by Von Beuren, debuts
Radio used for 1st time for a presidential campaign
Frans Slaats cycles a world hour record time (45,563 km)
1st archival course is offered at Columbia University in NYC
"Double or Nothing", a radio quiz show, 1st heard on Mutual Radio Net
Million jam downtown Brooklyn to cheer NL champ Dodgers in a parade
Nazi massacre at Babi Yar ravine (near Kiev) Ukraine begins, 33,771 mostly Jews murdered
32°F lowest temperature ever recorded in Cleveland, Ohio in September
French Government in exile of De Gaulle cancels agreement of Munich
1st Silbertanne-murder by German occupiers in Meppel
Zjadovs 5th Gardeleger/Managarovs 53rd Army reconquer Kremenchuk (Ukraine)
Browns last in AL attendance, only 6,172 watch them sweep Yanks in DH
Soviet troops invade Yugoslavia
Chicago Cubs clinch NL pennant with record of 98–56
"Adventures of Sam Spade" debuts on CBS Radio
1st time NL pennant ends in a tie (Cards & Dodgers)
Al Couture knockouts Ralph Walton in Lewiston Maine in 10 secs
Los Angeles (previously Cleveland) Rams play 1st NFL game in LA
NPS, Nationale Party Suriname, forms
Dizzy Gillespie presents his 1st Carnegie Hall concert, NYC
WBAP (now KXAS) TV channel 5 in Fort Worth-Dallas, TX (NBC) begins
WSB TV channel 2 in Atlanta, GA (ABC) begins broadcasting
"Front Page" debuts on CBS-TV
"Inside USA With Chevrolet" debuts on CBS-TV
"Tin Pan Alley" last airs on ABC TV
Telephone Answering Machine created by Bell Laboratories
American astronomer Seth Barnes Nicholson discovers Ananke, 12th moon of Jupiter from Mount Wilson Observatory, Los Angeles County, California
CBS broadcasts 1st football game in color, University of California against the University of Pennsylvania, at Philadelphia
Emile Zatopek runs world record 20,000 m & 10 mile
"Lights Out" last airs on NBC-TV
"Buick-Berle Show" debuts on NBC-TV
1st department store to sell insurance is Carson Pirie Scott in Chicago, Illinois
Baltimore, Maryland Mayor D'Alesandro buys Veeck's interest in Browns for $2,475,000
US government gives France $385 million for combat in Indo-China
"Masquerade Party" TV game show moves from CBS to ABC for two seasons
Bennekom (Netherlands) soccer team forms
KALB TV channel 5 in Alexandria, LA (NBC/ABC) begins broadcasting
The first remake of "A Star Is Born", starringJudy Garland& James Mason premieres at the Pantages Theatre, Los Angeles
"Sergeant Preston" debuts on CBS
Ali Sastroamidjojo's PNI wins elections in Indonesia
"Oh! Susanna" debuts on CBS-TV
Music single "I Love Mickey" byMickey Mantle& Teresa Brewer peaks at #87
"DuPont Show of the Month" debuts on CBS-TV
"The Paul Winchell Show" debuts on ABC-TV
An explosion at the Mayak plutonium production plant in the Soviet Union spreads radiation over 20,000 square miles (52,000 km2)
NFL NY Giants play their last game at Polo Grounds, 9-1 loss to Pittsburgh
Passenger train & oil train crash in Gambar, West Pakistan, killing 300
"Studio One" TV Anthology Drama last airs on CBS-TV
"Summertime Blues" by Eddie Cochran peaks at #8 in US; #18 in UK
"Texan" debuts on CBS-TV
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
"Many Loves Of Dobie Gillis" debuts on CBS-TV
"Philip Marlowe" debuts on ABC-TV
Dodgers win game 2 of playoff, 6-5, & take NL pennant
Little Anthony & the Imperials record "Shimmy Shimmy Ko Ko Bop"
Sultan of Brunei promulgates a constitution
"Johnny Ringo" TV Western Drama; last airs on CBS-TV
"My Three Sons" starring Fred MacMurray, debuts on ABC-TV
"Outlaws" TV Western Drama; debuts on NBC-TV
"Tell Laura I Love Her" by Ricky Valance peaks at #1 in UK
"Detectives" TV Crime Drama; moves to NBC-TV
Mamum Kuzbari becomes premier of Syria
"Alley Cat" by Bent Fabric & His Piano peaks at #7
"Green Onions" single by Booker T & MG's peaks at #3
"The Roy Rogers and Dale Evans Show" musical variety program debuts on ABC-TV; cancelled after 13 episodes
Launch of Alouette 1, 1st Canadian satellite (on US Delta rocket)
MLB Cleveland Indians and California Angels set AL record of 40 combined strikeouts in a doubleheader
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
"My Favorite Martian" starring Ray Walston, debuts on CBS-TV
"TheJudy GarlandShow" debuts on CBS-TV
2nd session of Ecumenical council, `Vatican II,' opened in Rome by Pope Paul
Houston Colt .45 John Paciorek goes 3 for 3 in his only game
Greece & Bulgaria close boundaries
Phillies catcher Pat Corrales sets record by reaching base twice on catcher's interference in one game & 6 times in one season
St L Cards Charlie Johnson passes for 6 touchdowns vs Cleve (49-13)
WLVT TV channel 39 in Allentown, PA (PBS) begins broadcasting
Chevrolet Camaro, originally named Panther, is introduced
-Oct 29] Rome: 1st bishop synod
International Monetary Fund reforms world monetary system
Mickey Hart joins the Grateful Dead for the 1st time, playing at the Straight Theater in San Francisco, California
Chuck Latourette, sets NFL record 47.7 yd punt return avg (3 punts)
"Bright Promise" TV Daytime Soap; debuts on NBC-TV
"Letters To Laugh-In" debuts on NBC-TV
"Love American Style" comedy anthology series premieres on ABC-TV
"Name Droppers" debuts on NBC-TV
"Sale Of Century" debuts on NBC-TV
China performs nuclear test at Lop Nor, Peoples Republic of China
Jackie DeShannon gets Gold Record for "Put Little Love in Your Heart"
MLB Red Sox Rico Petrocelli hits shortstop record 40th HR of season
"NET Festival" last aired
"McMillan & Wife" debuts on NBC-TV
Cyclone & tidal wave off Bay of Bengal kills as many as 10,000
Orbiting Solar Observatory VII launched
Ron Hunt is hit by a pitch for record 50th time in a season
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Japan & People's Republic of China begin diplomatic relations
"Jimmy Loves Mary-Anne" by Looking Glass peaks at #33
"We're An American Band" by Grand Funk peaks at #1
Pompey the Greatcelebrates his third triumph for victories over the pirates and the end of the Mithridatic Wars on his 45th birthday
War of Religion breaks out in France as Huguenots try to kidnap KingCharles IX
American oil tycoonJohn D. Rockefellerbecomes the world's first billionaire
Yankees sluggerBabe Ruthsets then-MLB home run season record at 54 with a 9th inning shot off Dave Keefe in New York's 7-3 win at the Philadelphia A's
Benito Mussoliniasks the Vatican for support of his fascist party program
Film "Bill of Divorcement" starringJohn BarrymoreandKatharine Hepburn, directed byGeorge Cukorpremieres at the Mayfair
1st US merchant ship "Booker T. Washington" commanded by a black captain (Hugh Mulzac), launched at Wilmington Delaware
Musical film "Strike Up the Band" starringMickey RooneyandJudy Garlandopens
Joe LouisTKOs Lou Nova in 6 for heavyweight boxing title
Former New York Yankees managerJoe McCarthysigns to manage Boston Red Sox
"Hamlet," directed by and starringLaurence Olivier, opens at Park Avenue Cinema (Best Picture 1949)
Elia Kazan's dramatic film "Pinky" starring Jeanne Crain,Ethel Barrymore, and Ethel Waters premieres at Rivoli Theater, NYC
Yanks clinch 2nd consecutive pennant underCasey Stengel
"Make Room for Daddy" starringDanny Thomas, premieres on ABC-TV
"Milton BerleShow" premieres on NBC in the US
"Barefoot Contessa" film directed by Joseph L. Mankiewicz, starringAva GardnerandHumphrey Bogartpremieres at the Capitol
NY Giants'Willie Maysmakes a famous over-the-shoulder catch of Cleveland Indians' Vic Wertz's 460' drive during Game One of the World Series at the Polo Grounds in NYC
Arthur Millers play "View From The Bridge" premieres in NYC
YanksMickey Mantlehits his 52nd HR of season
Buddy Holly& The Crickets release 2nd single "Oh Boy!"/"Not Fade Away"
Bob Dylan's 1st recording session, playing harmonica for Caroline Hester
Algerian government ofBen Bellaforms
JFKauthorizes use of federal troops to integrate University of Mississippi
Music single "You Can't Judge a Book by the Cover" byBo Diddleypeaks at #48
Cardinal's celebrateStan MusialDay in St Louis, for Stan Musial's final game, where he hits his 3,629th & 3,630th total hits (475 HRs)
Rolling Stones 1st tour, as opening act forBo Diddley& the Everly Brothers
Sandy Koufaxpitches 3rd 300-strikeout season
American boxerEmile Griffithregains his world middleweight crown on points in a brawling re-match with ItalianNino Benvenutiat Shea Stadium, NY; second of famous trilogy of fights
Gladys Knight& the Pips are the first to release Norman Whitfield and Barrett Strong's "I Heard It Through The Grapevine"
Boston Red Sox'sCarl Yastrzemski, with a .3005 average, wins his second straight batting crown

Robert McNamara, former US Secretary of Defense, is nearly thrown overboard on a ferry by an artist wanting to confront him about his role in escalating US involvement in the Vietnam War (no charges pressed)
First units of the London Metropolitan Police appear on the streets of the British capital, the city's first modern police force
Italy declares war on Turkey, starting the Italo-Turkish War
Margaret of England, Queen consort of Scotland, daughter of Henry III of England, born at Winsor Castle (d. 1275)
Joan of Kent [the Fair Maid of Kent], famous English beauty, wife of Edward the Black Prince, mother of Richard II, born at Palace of Woodstock, England (d. 1385)
Thomas of Lancaster, 1st Duke of Clarence, Medieval English prince and soldier, second son of Henry IV of England, born in London (d. 1421) [birthdate disputed]
Ferdinand, the Holy Prince, Portuguese prince, and prisoner of war at Fez, The Marinid Sultanate, born in Santarém, Portugal (d. 1443)
Michael Servetus, Spanish theologian and physician (Christianism Rostituta), born in Villanueva or Tudela, Spain (d. 1553)
Tintoretto, Italian Mannerist painter and notable exponent of the Renaissance school, born in Venice, Republic of Venice (d. 1594)
William V, German Duke of Bavaria (1579-97), known as 'the Pious', born in Landshut, Bavaria (d. 1626)
Adriaan van Roomen, Flemish mathematician, born in Leuven (d. 1615)
Caravaggio [Michelangelo Merisi da Caravaggio], Italian painter (Saint Francis of Assisi in Ecstasy) [birthdate disputed], born in Milan (d. 1610)
John VIII, the Younger, Count of Nassau-Siegen and field marshal, born in Dillenburg (d. 1638)
Jean Louis d'Elderen, 63rd Prince-Bishop of Liège (1688-94), born in Tongeren (modern Belgium) (d. 1694)
Thomas Tenison, English Archbishop of Canterbury (1694-1715), born in Cottenham, England (d. 1715)
William Russell, Lord Russell, English politician and plotter against Charles II, born in England (d. 1683)
Antoine Coyzevox, French Baroque sculptor (Palace of Versailles), born in Lyon, France (d. 1720) baptism date
Vincenz Lubeck, German composer, born in Padingbüttel, Duchy of Bremen, Sweden (d. 1740)
Jacques Hotteterre, French composer and flautist, born in Paris, France (d. 1762)
Adrien-Maurice de Noailles, third Duke of Noailles, soldier, and Marshal of France, born in Paris, Kingdom of France (d. 1766)[1]
Richard Challoner, English leading Catholic bishop (Douay–Rheims bible translation), born in Lewes, England (d. 1781)
François Boucher, French Rococo painter commissioned by Madame de Pompadour, born in Paris, France (d. 1770)
Peter Albertus van de Parra, Dutch Governor-general of Dutch East-Indies (1761-75), born in Colombo, Dutch Ceylon (d. 1775)
Nikita Ivanovich Panin, Russian statesman and advisor to Catherine the Great (plot against Peter III), born in Danzig (d. 1783)
Henry Harington, English composer and musician, born in Kelston, England (d. 1613)
Ernst Ludwig Gerber, German organist, composer and musicologist, born in Sondershausen, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1819)
Johann Gottfried Schicht, German composer, born in Reichenau, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1823)
William Beckford, British writer (Vathek), politician, plantation owner, and art collector, born in Soho Square, London (d. 1844)  [some sources give birth date as Oct 1]
William Michael Rooke, Irish composer and violinist, born in Dublin (d. 1847)
Charles-Francois Sturm, French mathematician (Sturm's Theorem), born in Geneva, Switzerland (d. 1855)
Mercator Cooper, American sea captain (first to visit Japan and Antarctica), born in Southampton, New York (d. 1872)
Elizabeth Gaskell, English novelist (North and South), born in London, England (d. 1865)
Adolph Göpel, German mathematician (hyperelliptic functions), born in Rostock (d. 1847)
Andreas Aschenbach, German Romantic painter and engraver, born in Kassel (d. 1910)
Bradley Tyler Johnson, American Brigadier General in the Confederate Army (First Battle of Winchester), born in Frederick City, Maryland (d. 1903)
Giles Alexander Smith, American Major General (Union Army), born in Jefferson County, New York (d. 1876)
John Parker Hawkins, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in Indianapolis, Indiana (d. 1914)
John McAlister Schofield, American Major General (Union Army), born in Gerry, New York (d. 1906)
Henry Hobson Richardson, American Romanesque revival architect (Thomas Crane Public Library), born in St James, Louisiana (d. 1886)
Louis J. Weichmann, American clerk who was the chief witness in the trial of the assassination of Abraham Lincoln, born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 1902)
Mikhail Skobelev 'the White General', Russian general during Russo-Turkish War, born in Saint Petersburg, Russia (d. 1882)
Caroline Yale, American educator who revolutionized the teaching of hearing-impaired students, born in Charlotte, Vermont (d. 1943)
Ladislao Zavertal, Czech-Italian conductor and composer, born in Milan, Italy (d. 1942)
Princess Thyra, Danish princess, daughter of Christian IX, born at the Yellow Palace, Copenhagen (d. 1933)
Martin Pluddemann, German composer, born in Kolberg, Pomerania, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1897)
Michele Esposito, Italian composer who worked in Ireland, born in Castellamare di Stabia (d. 1929)
Conrad Theodor van Deventer, Dutch author, lawyer and politician (Ethical Policy Movement), born in Dordrecht, Netherlands (d. 1915)
Leopoldo Mugnone, Italian opera composer and conductor, born in Naples, Kingdom of the Two Sicilie (d. 1941)
Franz Zorell, German lexicographer and exegete (Hebrew Latin Dictionary), born in Ravensburg (d. 1947)
Hugo Haase, German socialist politician (SPD, USPD) and co-chair of the Council of the People's Deputies (1918-19), born in Allenstein, East Prussia (d. 1919)
Miguel de Unamuno, Spanish writer and philosopher (Ensayos, Teatro), born in Bilbao Spain (d. 1936)
Mykhailo Hrushevsky, Ukrainian historian and statesman, born in Chełm, Congress Poland (d. 1934)
Joaquín Nin, Cuban composer and pianist, father of Anaïs Nin, born in Havana, Cuba (d. 1949)
Ludwig von Mises, Austrian-American Economist (The Anti-Capitalistic Mentality), born in Lemberg, Austria-Hungary (d. 1973)
Boris Asafiev, Russian composer and music critic, born in Saint Petersburg, Russia (d. 1949)
Billy Bevan, Australian actor (Terror by Night, White Sin), born in Orange, New South Wales, Australia (d. 1957)
Fabien Sevitzky [Koussevitzky], Russian-American conductor (Indianapolis Symphony), born in Vishny Volotchok, Russia (d. 1967)
Gerda Björne, Swedish actress (Familjen Bjork, Hemsoborna), born in By, Värmland, Sweden (d. 1979)
Phillips Tead, American character actor (The Front Page; Lightnin'; Fighting Blade; The Adventures of Superman - "Professor Pepperwinkle"), born in Somerville, Massachusetts (d. 1974)
Franco Capuana, Italian composer, born in Fano, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1969)
Joseph Banks Rhine, American parapsychologist (Extra-Sensory Perception), born in Waterloo, Pennsylvania (d. 1980)
Louise Granville, Australian actress (How Green Paid the Rent), born in Sydney, New South Wales (d. 1968)
Roscoe Turner, American aviator, air racer and founder of Turner airlines, born in Corinth, Mississippi (d. 1970)
Štefan Németh-Šamorínsky, Slovak organist, choirmaster, composer and pedagogue (Birch Trees), born in Šamorín, Slovakia, Kingdom of Hungary (d. 1975)
Herbert Agar, American journalist and historian (Pulitzer Prize, 1934 - The People's Choice), born in New Rochelle, New York (d. 1980)
Trofim Lysenko, Soviet biologist and agronomist, born in Karlovka, Ukraine, Russian Empire (d. 1976)
Billy Butlin, South African-British holiday camp promoter (Butlins), born in Cape Town, Cape Colony (d. 1980)
Lásló Jósef Bíró, Hungarian inventor (ballpoint pen), born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 1985)
Miguel Alemán Valdés, Mexican attorney and President of Mexico (1946-52), born in Sayula, Veracruz, Mexico (d. 1983)
Lanza del Vasto, Italian philosopher and nonviolent activist, born in San Vito dei Normanni, Italy (d. 1981)
(Edward) "Ted" de Corsia, American actor (The Naked City; 20,000 Leagues Under the Sea), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1973)
Diana Vreeland, French-American fashion editor (Harper's Bazaar; Vogue), born in Paris (d. 1989)
Karl August Andersen, Norwegian cellist and composer, born in Oslo, Norway (d. 1970)
Dorothy Emmet, English philosopher, born in London, England (d. 2000)
Fidel LaBarba, American boxer (Olympic gold flyweight 1924; world flyweight title 1927), born in New York City (d. 1981)

Gene Autry, American cowboy singer, songwriter, actor, musician, ("Back In The Saddle Again"; "Rudolph The Red-Nosed Reindeer") and business tycoon (owner MLB Angels team, 1961-97), born near Tioga, Texas (d. 1998)
Michael Shepley, British actor (Squibs, Dick & the Duchess), born in Plymouth, Devon, England (d. 1961)
Richard Harkness, American newscaster (Story of the Week, NBC), born in Artesian, South Dakota (d. 1977)
Eddie Tolan, American sprinter (2 Golds 1932 Olympics), born in Denver, Colorado (d. 1967)
Norman Anderson, English lawyer, missionary and Arabist, born in Aldeburgh, England (d. 1994)
Sashadhar Mukherjee, Indian film producer (Jagrit), born in Jhansi, India (d. 1990)
Bill Boyd, American western swing guitarist and band leader (The Cowboy Ramblers - ”Under the Double Eagle"), born near Ladonia, Texas (d. 2001)
Virginia Bruce, American actress (Action in Arabia), born in Minneapolis, Minnesota (d. 1982)
Reginald Victor Jones, British scientist who worked in military intelligence in WWII, born in London, England (d. 1997)
Lukas Ammann, Swiss actor (Graf Yoster; Day of Anger; Class Reunion; Palace Hotel), born in Basel, Switzerland (d. 2017)
Michelangelo Antonioni, Italian director (Blowup, Red Desert), born in Ferrara, Italy (d. 2007)
Silvio Piola, Italian soccer striker (34 caps, 30 goals; Lazio), born in Robbio Lomellina, Pavia (d. 1996)
Stanley E. Kramer, American producer and director (Inherit the Wind), born in New York City (d. 2001)
Ann Nagel [Anna Marie Dolan], American actress (Don Winslow of the Navy, The Green Hornet), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1966)
Brenda Marshall [Ardis Ankerson], American actress (The Sea Hawk, Paris After Dark), born in Island of Negros, Philippines (d. 1992)
Vincent DeDomenico, American entrepreneur (Rice-A-Roni), born in San Francisco, California (d. 2007)
Carl Ronald Giles, English cartoonist (Giles), born in London, England (d. 1995)
Minao Shibata, Japanese composer and musicologist, born in Tokyo (d. 1996)
Don Castle, American actor (Born to Speed; The Invisible Wall), and television producer (Lassie), born in Beaumont, Texas (d. 1966)
Harold Laurence Walters, American composer, born in Gurdon, Arkansas (d. 1984)
Masao Takemoto, Japanese gymnast (Olympic gold team 1960; 3 x silver 1952, 56, 60; 3 x bronze 1956), born in Hamada, Japan (d. 2007)
Nancy Leftenant-Colon, American Army nurse and major, first Black woman to join US Army Corps in 1946, born in Goose Creek, South Carolina (d. 2025)[1]
Peter D. Mitchell, English chemist, (1978 Nobel Prize for Chemistry), born in Mitcham, England (d. 1992)
Tillman Franks, American country music bassist, songwriter and artist manager (Webb Pierce), born in Stamps, Arkansas (d. 2006)
Václav Neummann, Czech conductor and musician, born in Prague, Czechoslovakia (d. 1995)
Miklós Jancsó, Hungarian director (Muzsika, Aube; Pacifist), born in Vac, Hungary (d. 2014)
Lizabeth Scott [Emma Matzo], American actress (Dark City, Desert Fury), born in Scranton, Pennsylvania (d. 2015)
Bum Phillips, American football coach (Houston Oilers, New Orleans Saints), born in Orange, Texas (d. 2013)
Stan Berenstain, American children's author (The Berenstain Bears), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2005)
David Atkinson, British air marshal, and honorary physician to the Queen Elizabeth II, born in Hartlepool, England (d. 2013)
John Tower, American politician (Senate-R-Tx 1961-85), born in Houston, Texas (d. 1991)
Steve Forrest, American actor (Ben-Dallas, SWAT), born in Huntsville, Texas (d. 2013)
Chuck Cooper, American shooting guard and 1st African American to be drafted into the NBA (Boston Celtics), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1984)
Adhemar da Silva, Brazilian triple jumper (Olympic golds 1952, 56), born in São Paulo, Brazil (d. 2001)
Pete McCloskey, American politician (US Representative from California, 1967-83), born in Loma Linda, California (d. 2024)
Ankie Peypers, Dutch author and poet, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 2008)
Eric Lubbock, Lord Avebury, English politician and human rights advocate (Liberal MP for Orpington 1962-70), born in Orpington, England (d. 2016)
Valentinas Bagdonas, Lithuanian composer (Symphony of Peace), born in Sriūbos, Lithuania (d. 2009)
Billy Strange [William], American singer-songwriter (A Little Less Conversation), born in Long Beach, California (d. 2012)
Colin Dexter, English crime writer (Inspector Morse), born in Stamford, Lincolnshire (d. 2017)
Peter Miller, British barrister and executive (Chairman of Lloyd's Bank of London, 1984-90), born in London (d. 2018)
Ramnath Kenny, Indian cricketer (batted in 5 Tests for India in 1950's), born in Bombay, British India (d. 1985)
Richard Bonynge, Australian opera conductor (Dame Joan Sutherland, 1962-90; Metropolitan Opera, 1966-91; Vancouver Opera, 1974-82), born in Epping, New South Wales, Australia
Eddie Barth [Edward Bartholetti], American actor and voiceover artist nicknamed "Mr. Gravel" for his raspy vocals (Shaft, Simon & Simon), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2010)
James Cronin, American nuclear physicist (Physics Nobel Prize 1980), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2016)
Joseph M. McDade, American politician (Rep-R-Pennsylvania, 1963-1999), born in Scranton, Pennsylvania (d. 2017)
Jeremy Isaacs, Scottish TV executive (Channel 4) and opera company manager (Royal Opera House, Covent Garden, 1987-96), born in Glasgow, Scotland
Mehmood [Ali], Indian actor, singer, director and producer (Do Phoo!, Vardaan), born in Bombay, British India (d. 2004)
Peter Stormonth Darling, Scottish investment banker (Mercury Asset Management Group, largest fund-management in the UK), born in Scotland (d. 2019)
Rainer Weiss, German-American physicist known for his work on the LIGO detector (Nobel Prize, 2017), born in Berlin, Germany (d. 2025)[1]
Robert Benton, American screenwriter and director (Kramer vs Kramer), born in Waxahachie, Texas (d. 2025)
Ronald B. Kitaj, American-British painter and graphic artist (pop art), born in Chagrin Falls, Ohio (d. 2007)
Samora Machel, President of Mozambique (1975-86), born in Gaza Province, Mozambique (d. 1986)
Lindsay Kline, Australian cricketer (Australian chinaman bowler Hat-trick 1957), born in Melbourne, Australia (d. 2015)
Mihály Csíkszentmihályi, Hungarian- American psychologist (concept of "flow"), born in Rijeka, Kingdom of Italy (d. 2021)
David Hannay, British diplomat (UK representative to the UN and EU), born in London, England
